name: Middle-earth Lore: Shire to Silmarillion
description: Tolkien's legendarium in 132 cards, working backward through time: the War of the Ring, deep Third Age history, the forging of the Rings of Power and the fall of Numenor, and finally the First Age of the Silmarillion - the Valar, Feanor, Beren and Luthien, and the overthrow of Morgoth.

fields.front: The fiery inscription on the One Ring is written in what language?
fields.back: The Black Speech of Mordor
fields.extra: The letters themselves are Tengwar, the Elvish script. Gandalf recites the verse aloud in Black Speech at the Council of Elrond, and the sky darkens.

fields.front: In the Ring-verse, how many Rings of Power went to the Elves, the Dwarves, and Men?
fields.back: Three for the Elven-kings, Seven for the Dwarf-lords, Nine for Mortal Men - and One for the Dark Lord
fields.extra: Nineteen lesser rings plus the One. The fates of the Three, Seven, and Nine diverged sharply - later cards cover each.

fields.front: Name the nine members of the Fellowship of the Ring.
fields.back: Frodo, Sam, Merry, Pippin, Gandalf, Aragorn, Boromir, Legolas, and Gimli
fields.extra: Nine walkers set against the nine Ringwraiths. Four hobbits, two Men, a wizard, an elf, and a dwarf.

fields.front: What is Frodo's family relationship to Bilbo?
fields.back: His cousin - Bilbo adopted him as his heir
fields.extra: Not his nephew, despite what Bilbo calls him. Frodo's parents, Drogo Baggins and Primula Brandybuck, drowned in a boating accident when he was twelve.

fields.front: What was Gollum's name before he found the Ring, and what kind of creature was he?
fields.back: Smeagol, a hobbit of the river-folk (akin to the Stoors)
fields.extra: He lived near the Gladden Fields on the Anduin and kept the Ring for nearly 500 years under the Misty Mountains.

fields.front: Who actually found the One Ring in the river Anduin - and what happened to him?
fields.back: Deagol, Smeagol's friend; Smeagol strangled him for it
fields.extra: It was Smeagol's birthday, and he demanded the Ring as a present. The murder was the Ring's first act of corruption in its long dormancy.

fields.front: What is the name of Frodo's sword, and what does it do near orcs?
fields.back: Sting - it glows blue when orcs are near
fields.extra: Bilbo found it in a troll-hoard and passed it to Frodo in Rivendell. It is an Elvish blade forged in Gondolin in the First Age.

fields.front: What is the name of Gandalf's sword?
fields.back: Glamdring, the Foe-hammer
fields.extra: Found in the same troll-hoard as Sting. It was forged in Gondolin and once borne by Turgon, the city's king - Gandalf carries a First Age royal blade.

fields.front: What hidden armor saves Frodo from a spear-thrust in Moria?
fields.back: His mithril coat, a gift from Bilbo
fields.extra: Originally given to Bilbo by Thorin from Smaug's hoard. Gandalf said its worth was greater than the value of the whole Shire.

fields.front: Aragorn is the heir of which ancient king, giving him a claim to Gondor's throne?
fields.back: Isildur
fields.extra: Aragorn son of Arathorn is Chieftain of the Dunedain of the North, descended from Isildur through the kings of Arnor in unbroken line.

fields.front: What sword is reforged for Aragorn from the shards of Narsil, and what is it called?
fields.back: Anduril, the Flame of the West
fields.extra: Narsil broke under Elendil when he fell fighting Sauron; the elves of Rivendell reforged the shards for the War of the Ring.

fields.front: What is the Elvish name of Mount Doom?
fields.back: Orodruin
fields.extra: Also called Amon Amarth ('Mount of Fate'). It is the only fire hot enough to unmake the One Ring, because the Ring was forged there.

fields.front: What is the Elvish name of Rivendell, and who is its master?
fields.back: Imladris, ruled by Elrond
fields.extra: The Last Homely House East of the Sea. Elrond is Half-elven, and his history stretches back to the First Age - much more on him later.

fields.front: Who rule the golden wood of Lothlorien?
fields.back: Galadriel and Celeborn
fields.extra: Its capital is Caras Galadhon, built in the crowns of the mallorn trees. Galadriel is among the oldest and greatest elves left in Middle-earth.

fields.front: Who are the Nazgul?
fields.back: The nine Ringwraiths - mortal kings and sorcerers enslaved by the Nine Rings Sauron gave to Men
fields.extra: Nazgul is Black Speech for 'ring-wraith'. They are neither living nor dead, bound to the One Ring and to Sauron's will.

fields.front: The lord of the Nazgul once ruled a northern realm as what king?
fields.back: The Witch-king of Angmar
fields.extra: In the mid-Third Age his realm of Angmar destroyed the North-kingdom of Arnor - which is why Aragorn's people survived only as wandering Rangers.

fields.front: Who kills the Witch-king at the Battle of the Pelennor Fields?
fields.back: Eowyn of Rohan, after Merry stabs him from behind
fields.extra: Answering the prophecy that 'not by the hand of man' would he fall: 'No living man am I! You look upon a woman.'

fields.front: Why could Merry's sword wound the Witch-king when other weapons failed?
fields.back: It was a barrow-blade of Westernesse, forged in Arnor long ago for war against Angmar
fields.extra: Merry took it from the Barrow-downs. Tolkien: no other blade would have dealt that foe a wound so bitter, breaking the spell knitting his sinews.

fields.front: Who is King of Rohan during the War of the Ring, and where is his hall?
fields.back: Theoden son of Thengel; his golden hall Meduseld stands in Edoras
fields.extra: Freed from Saruman's influence by Gandalf, he dies on the Pelennor Fields, crushed beneath his horse Snowmane.

fields.front: How are Eomer and Eowyn related to King Theoden?
fields.back: They are his sister's children - his nephew and niece
fields.extra: Children of Theodwyn and Eomund, adopted into Theoden's house after their parents died. Eomer becomes king after Theoden falls.

fields.front: What is the name of the fortress defended at Helm's Deep, and who is the valley named after?
fields.back: The Hornburg, in the valley named for Helm Hammerhand
fields.extra: Helm was a legendary king of Rohan who, in a bitter winter siege, stalked his enemies alone in the snow. The horn blown before his wraith-like raids gave the Hornburg its name.

fields.front: What is the name of Saruman's tower at Isengard?
fields.back: Orthanc
fields.extra: Built by the men of Gondor in the Second Age, not by Saruman - he was merely its keeper. Its unbreakable black stone survives the Ents' assault.

fields.front: What are the palantiri?
fields.back: The seeing-stones: crystal globes that let their users see far off and communicate with other stones
fields.extra: Saruman and Denethor were both ensnared through them, because Sauron held one himself - the Ithil-stone taken at Minas Morgul.

fields.front: What is Treebeard, and what is the Elvish name for his kind?
fields.back: An Ent - the Onodrim, shepherds of the trees
fields.extra: Treebeard's own Elvish name is Fangorn, the same as his forest. Gandalf calls him the oldest living thing that still walks beneath the Sun in Middle-earth.

fields.front: Boromir and Faramir are the sons of which ruler?
fields.back: Denethor II, Ruling Steward of Gondor
fields.extra: Boromir falls at Amon Hen defending Merry and Pippin from orc-arrows. Denethor rules Gondor but is not its king - a distinction covered soon.

fields.front: How is the city of Minas Tirith built?
fields.back: In seven walled levels carved into the mountain, crowned by the White Tower of Ecthelion
fields.extra: Each gate faces a different direction so no enemy can charge straight up. The Court of the Fountain at the top holds the White Tree.

fields.front: What is Shelob, the monster of Cirith Ungol?
fields.back: A great spider - the last child of Ungoliant, an ancient darkness from before the First Age
fields.extra: Sam drives her off with Sting and the Phial of Galadriel. Her mother Ungoliant appears near the end of this deck.

fields.front: What is special about Shadowfax, Gandalf's horse?
fields.back: He is lord of the Mearas, the greatest horses of Rohan, and bears no other rider
fields.extra: The Mearas descend from Felarof, tamed by Eorl the Young, and by tradition carried only the kings of the Mark.

fields.front: Which great Eagle twice rescues Gandalf, and carries him from the peak after the Balrog fight?
fields.back: Gwaihir the Windlord
fields.extra: He also plucks Frodo and Sam from the slopes of Mount Doom. The Eagles are servants of Manwe, lord of the Valar - not a taxi service.

fields.front: Who are the fathers of Legolas and Gimli?
fields.back: Legolas is son of Thranduil, Elvenking of Mirkwood; Gimli is son of Gloin of Thorin's company
fields.extra: Both fathers appear in The Hobbit: Thranduil imprisoned the dwarves, and Gloin was one of them - a feud their sons famously bury.

fields.front: Why could Barad-dur never be wholly destroyed while the One Ring survived?
fields.back: Its foundations were made with the power of the Ring
fields.extra: After the Last Alliance razed the tower, the foundations endured, and Sauron rebuilt it. Only the Ring's destruction brought it down for good.

fields.front: What is the Elvish name of the Grey Havens, and who is its shipwright lord?
fields.back: Mithlond, ruled by Cirdan the Shipwright
fields.extra: From here the elves sail west out of Middle-earth. Cirdan is among the oldest elves alive, ancient already when the First Age began.

fields.front: Name the five Istari (wizards) sent to Middle-earth.
fields.back: Saruman the White, Gandalf the Grey, Radagast the Brown, and the two Blue Wizards, Alatar and Pallando
fields.extra: They arrived around Third Age 1000. The Blue Wizards went into the far East and never return in the tales.

fields.front: What kind of beings are the wizards, really?
fields.back: Maiar - angelic spirits clothed in the bodies of old men, sent to rally resistance to Sauron
fields.extra: They were forbidden to match Sauron's power with power or dominate the free peoples - the rule Saruman broke and Gandalf kept.

fields.front: What do the Elves call Gandalf, and what was his name in the West before he was sent to Middle-earth?
fields.back: The Elves call him Mithrandir ('Grey Pilgrim'); in Valinor he was the Maia Olorin
fields.extra: Also Tharkun to the Dwarves and Incanus in the South. 'Gandalf' is just his name among Men of the North.

fields.front: Name the Three Elven Rings and their bearers during the War of the Ring.
fields.back: Narya (fire) borne by Gandalf, Nenya (water/adamant) by Galadriel, Vilya (air) by Elrond
fields.extra: Cirdan surrendered Narya to Gandalf on his arrival, seeing what he truly was. Vilya is accounted the mightiest of the Three.

fields.front: What do the Dwarves call the realm Men and Elves call Moria?
fields.back: Khazad-dum, the mansion of the Dwarves
fields.extra: Moria is Elvish for 'black chasm' - a name of dread given after its fall. It was the greatest dwarf-realm, founded by Durin the Deathless.

fields.front: What is Durin's Bane, and how was it awakened?
fields.back: The Balrog of Moria, woken when the Dwarves mined too deep for mithril
fields.extra: It killed King Durin VI in Third Age 1980 and emptied Khazad-dum. Balrogs are corrupted Maiar of fire, servants of Morgoth from the First Age.

fields.front: After falling with the Balrog in Moria, where did Gandalf finally slay it?
fields.back: On the peak of Zirakzigil, atop Durin's Tower, after fighting from the deepest tunnels up the Endless Stair
fields.extra: Gandalf died from the ordeal and was sent back 'until his task was done' - returning as Gandalf the White.

fields.front: Where in Middle-earth could mithril be found?
fields.back: Only in Khazad-dum (Moria)
fields.extra: True-silver: light as a feather, hard as dragon-scales, never tarnishing. Its rarity made it priceless - and mining it woke the Balrog.

fields.front: How did Isildur die, and how was the One Ring lost for an age?
fields.back: Orcs ambushed him at the Gladden Fields; the Ring slipped from his finger as he swam the Anduin, and he was shot
fields.extra: The Ring betrayed him to its own advantage - a pattern it repeats. It lay in the riverbed for roughly 2,500 years until Deagol found it.

fields.front: Why is Gondor ruled by Stewards instead of kings when the War of the Ring begins?
fields.back: The last king, Earnur, rode to Minas Morgul to answer the Witch-king's challenge and never returned
fields.extra: From Third Age 2050 the Stewards ruled 'until the king returns' - nearly a thousand years. Denethor II is the last Ruling Steward.

fields.front: What were Minas Morgul and Minas Tirith originally called?
fields.back: Minas Ithil (Tower of the Moon) and Minas Anor (Tower of the Sun)
fields.extra: When the Nazgul captured Minas Ithil in Third Age 2002 it became Minas Morgul, tower of sorcery; Minas Anor was renamed Minas Tirith, tower of guard.

fields.front: What city was Gondor's original capital, straddling the Anduin?
fields.back: Osgiliath, the Citadel of the Stars
fields.extra: Ruined by plague and war long before the War of the Ring; by Frodo's day it is a contested crossing, not a city.

fields.front: The Argonath, the giant river-gate statues on the Anduin, depict which two figures?
fields.back: Isildur and Anarion, the sons of Elendil
fields.extra: The Pillars of the Kings marked Gondor's ancient northern border. Aragorn passes between images of his own ancestors.

fields.front: How did the Rohirrim come to possess Rohan?
fields.back: Eorl the Young rode to Gondor's rescue at the Field of Celebrant, and the Steward Cirion granted his people the land of Calenardhon
fields.extra: The Oath of Eorl bound Rohan and Gondor in mutual aid forever - the oath Theoden answers when the beacons are lit.

fields.front: What was Aragorn called as a child in Rivendell, and under what name did he serve Rohan and Gondor in his youth?
fields.back: Estel ('Hope') as a child; Thorongil ('Eagle of the Star') in disguise
fields.extra: As king he takes the name Elessar ('Elfstone') and founds the house of Telcontar - 'Strider' rendered into high speech.

fields.front: How is Arwen related to Elrond and to Galadriel?
fields.back: She is Elrond's daughter, and Galadriel's granddaughter through her mother Celebrian
fields.extra: Called Undomiel, the Evenstar of her people. Like her father she is Half-elven, and must choose between elven immortality and mortal life.

fields.front: Who was 'the Necromancer' of Dol Guldur in The Hobbit?
fields.back: Sauron, secretly regathering strength in southern Mirkwood
fields.extra: The White Council drove him out in the same year as Bilbo's quest - but he simply withdrew, openly declaring himself in Mordor soon after.

fields.front: Who was chosen to lead the White Council, and whom did Galadriel want instead?
fields.back: Saruman led it; Galadriel wished for Gandalf
fields.extra: Gandalf refused all bonds and rank. Saruman's resentment of Gandalf, and his secret ring-lore studies, began his fall.

fields.front: What was Mirkwood called before the Shadow fell on it?
fields.back: Greenwood the Great
fields.extra: It darkened when Sauron settled at Dol Guldur. After the War of the Ring it is cleansed and renamed Eryn Lasgalen, the Wood of Greenleaves.

fields.front: Who killed the dragon Smaug, and how?
fields.back: Bard the Bowman of Lake-town, with the Black Arrow through the bare patch in Smaug's jeweled underbelly
fields.extra: A thrush carried word of the weak spot Bilbo had discovered. Bard, a descendant of the lords of Dale, rebuilt that city as its king.

fields.front: What became of the Seven Rings given to the Dwarf-lords?
fields.back: Dragons devoured four; Sauron recovered the other three - but the Dwarves never became wraiths
fields.extra: Dwarves proved too tough to enslave; the rings only inflamed their gold-lust. The last, Thrain's, was taken from him in the dungeons of Dol Guldur.

fields.front: What happens when Tom Bombadil puts on the One Ring?
fields.back: Nothing - it has no power over him, and he does not vanish
fields.extra: He calls himself Eldest, 'here before the river and the trees'. His wife is Goldberry, the River-daughter. Cut from every adaptation, to fans' eternal grief.

fields.front: In the book, which elf-lord rescues Frodo at the Ford of Bruinen (replaced by Arwen in the film)?
fields.back: Glorfindel
fields.extra: The flood itself was Elrond's doing. Glorfindel has one of the strangest histories of any elf in Middle-earth - his story resurfaces in the First Age cards.

fields.front: In the book, who is 'Sharkey', the ruffian boss the hobbits overthrow in the Scouring of the Shire?
fields.back: Saruman, petty and diminished, taking revenge on the hobbits
fields.extra: Wormtongue finally turns and kills him on Bag End's doorstep. The chapter was omitted entirely from the films.

fields.front: What happened to Arnor, the North-kingdom of the Dunedain?
fields.back: It split into three realms and was destroyed piecemeal by the Witch-king of Angmar
fields.extra: Its heirs survived as the Rangers of the North, chieftained by Aragorn's line, secretly guarding lands that forgot them - including the Shire.

fields.front: Which mortals were granted passage over the Sea into the West?
fields.back: Bilbo and Frodo (with Gandalf, Elrond, and Galadriel); Sam years later; and Gimli, the only dwarf, sailing with Legolas
fields.extra: The three Ring-bearers went first. Passage brought healing, not immortality - mortals remain mortal even in the Undying Lands.

fields.front: Why are there no Ent children?
fields.back: The Entwives were lost - their gardens were destroyed in the wars of the Second Age, and the Ents never found them again
fields.extra: Treebeard says the Ents loved the wild woods and the Entwives loved ordered gardens; they simply drifted apart, then the gardens became the Brown Lands.

fields.front: What was Numenor, and why was it given to Men?
fields.back: A great island raised in the western sea, the Valar's reward to the Edain for fighting Morgoth in the First Age
fields.extra: Also called Westernesse, Andor ('Land of Gift'), and Elenna. Numenoreans were granted long life - thrice that of other men - but not immortality.

fields.front: Who was the first King of Numenor, and who was his twin brother?
fields.back: Elros Tar-Minyatur - twin brother of Elrond
fields.extra: Both Half-elven, the twins were given a choice: Elros chose mortality and lived 500 years; Elrond chose the Elves. Aragorn descends from Elros - making Elrond a distant ancestor-in-law.

fields.front: What stood at the summit of the Meneltarma, the holy mountain at Numenor's center?
fields.back: The Hallow of Eru Iluvatar - an open sanctuary to the One, where no tool or weapon was ever brought
fields.extra: The only place of worship in Numenor, and the only place where the King spoke aloud there, three times a year.

fields.front: Where were the Rings of Power forged, and by whom?
fields.back: In Eregion, by the elven-smiths of the Gwaith-i-Mirdan led by Celebrimbor
fields.extra: Eregion ('Hollin') lay near Moria's west-gate - elves and dwarves were friends there, a rarity. The Fellowship walks its ruins.

fields.front: Under what fair disguise did Sauron deceive the elven-smiths of Eregion?
fields.back: As Annatar, 'Lord of Gifts', a beautiful emissary teaching ring-craft
fields.extra: Gil-galad and Elrond distrusted him and barred him from Lindon, but Celebrimbor's smiths welcomed his knowledge.

fields.front: Celebrimbor, forger of the Three Elven Rings, was the grandson of which infamous First Age elf?
fields.back: Feanor, maker of the Silmarils
fields.extra: The craft-obsession ran in the family - and so did the doom. Celebrimbor was slain when Sauron sacked Eregion, his body hung on a pole as a banner.

fields.front: How did the Elves learn of Sauron's treachery the moment the One Ring was forged?
fields.back: The One was made to dominate the other Rings - the instant Sauron put it on, Celebrimbor and the elves perceived him and took off their rings
fields.extra: Sauron forged the One alone in the fires of Orodruin, pouring much of his own native power into it - his great strength and his fatal weakness.

fields.front: Why were the Three Elven Rings safe to use after Sauron lost the One?
fields.back: Celebrimbor forged them alone - Sauron never touched them - though they were still bound to the One's power
fields.extra: While Sauron held the One they lay hidden and unused. If the One were destroyed, their power too would fade - the price Elrond and Galadriel knowingly accept.

fields.front: During which war was Rivendell founded?
fields.back: The War of the Elves and Sauron - Elrond founded it as a refuge after Eregion was destroyed
fields.extra: Second Age 1697. Sauron overran Eregion and nearly conquered all Eriador before a Numenorean army crushed him - Numenor at the height of its power.

fields.front: Which is the only Nazgul besides the Witch-king who is ever named?
fields.back: Khamul the Easterling, second of the Nine
fields.extra: He kept Dol Guldur after Sauron left it. The Nine first appeared around Second Age 2251, some thousand years after Men received their rings.

fields.front: How did Sauron come to Numenor?
fields.back: King Ar-Pharazon landed with so vast an army that Sauron's forces deserted - so Sauron surrendered and was taken to Numenor as a hostage
fields.extra: Humiliation as strategy: within a few years the prisoner was the king's closest counselor. Ar-Pharazon was Numenor's 25th and last king.

fields.front: What religion did Sauron establish in Numenor?
fields.back: Worship of Melkor (Morgoth), with human sacrifice in a great temple - promising the king escape from death
fields.extra: The fear of death was Numenor's oldest wound; Sauron merely poisoned it. The white tree Nimloth was burned on the temple's first altar.

fields.front: How did the line of Gondor's White Tree survive the burning of Nimloth in Numenor?
fields.back: Isildur stole a fruit from the tree before it was destroyed, at great cost to himself
fields.extra: He crept past the guards and was nearly killed by his wounds. Nimloth's line descends from an image of Telperion, eldest of the Two Trees of Valinor.

fields.front: What did Amandil, Elendil's father, attempt when Numenor turned to darkness?
fields.back: He sailed west to plead with the Valar, as Earendil once had - and was never heard from again
fields.extra: Leader of the Faithful, the party that stayed loyal to the Elves and the Valar. His counsel to Elendil: prepare ships and hold apart.

fields.front: What happened when Ar-Pharazon's armada landed on the shores of the Undying Lands?
fields.back: The Valar laid down their guardianship and called on Eru, who drowned Numenor and removed the Undying Lands from the circles of the world
fields.extra: The world was bent into a round world; only elven-ships may still find the Straight Road west. Ar-Pharazon's host lies buried until the Last Battle.

fields.front: What does 'Akallabeth' mean, and what is its famous Quenya equivalent?
fields.back: 'The Downfallen' - in Quenya, Atalante
fields.extra: Tolkien's deliberate echo of Atlantis. The Akallabeth is the fourth part of The Silmarillion, telling Numenor's whole rise and ruin.

fields.front: Who escaped the Downfall of Numenor, and in how many ships?
fields.back: Elendil and his sons Isildur and Anarion, with the Faithful, in nine ships
fields.extra: Four for Elendil, three for Isildur, two for Anarion. They carried a seedling of Nimloth and the seven palantiri.

fields.front: What two kingdoms did the exiles of Numenor found in Middle-earth?
fields.back: Arnor in the north under Elendil, and Gondor in the south under Isildur and Anarion jointly
fields.extra: Elendil was High King over both. Every 'return of the king' plot thread in The Lord of the Rings traces back to this arrangement.

fields.front: Who originally made the palantiri?
fields.back: Feanor, it is said, in the Undying Lands in the deeps of time
fields.extra: The Eldar gave them to the Faithful of Numenor; Elendil brought seven to Middle-earth. The master-stone remained in the West.

fields.front: Who led the Last Alliance of Elves and Men against Sauron?
fields.back: Gil-galad, High King of the Elves, and Elendil, High King of the Dunedain
fields.extra: The greatest host mustered since the War of Wrath. Elrond was Gil-galad's herald; the opening battle of the film compresses this war.

fields.front: What title did Gil-galad hold, and what was his famous weapon?
fields.back: Last High King of the Noldor in Middle-earth; his spear Aeglos ('Snow-point')
fields.extra: Sam recites part of 'The Fall of Gil-galad' on Weathertop. After his death the Noldor never appointed another High King.

fields.front: How long did the Last Alliance besiege Barad-dur, and which son of Elendil died there?
fields.back: Seven years; Anarion was slain by a stone cast from the tower
fields.extra: Before the siege came the Battle of Dagorlad, fought on the plain that became the Dead Marshes - the faces Frodo sees in the water.

fields.front: Who finally brought Sauron down at the end of the Second Age - and at what cost?
fields.back: Gil-galad and Elendil overthrew him in single combat on the slopes of Orodruin, but both were killed
fields.extra: Narsil broke beneath Elendil as he fell. Isildur cut the Ring from Sauron's hand with the hilt-shard of his father's sword.

fields.front: Why did Isildur refuse to destroy the One Ring when Elrond and Cirdan urged it?
fields.back: He claimed it as weregild - a blood-price for his father and brother
fields.extra: 'This I will have as weregild for my father, and my brother.' Two years later it betrayed him at the Gladden Fields.

fields.front: Who edited and published The Silmarillion after J.R.R. Tolkien's death?
fields.back: His son Christopher Tolkien, in 1977 (assisted by Guy Gavriel Kay)
fields.extra: Tolkien worked on these legends from 1917 until his death in 1973 - they predate The Hobbit by two decades. Everything from here on comes from that deeper well.

fields.front: Who is the supreme creator in Tolkien's legendarium?
fields.back: Eru Iluvatar, the One
fields.extra: 'Eru' means The One; 'Iluvatar' means Father of All. He alone possesses the Flame Imperishable, the power to give true independent life.

fields.front: How was the world created in the Ainulindale?
fields.back: Eru and the Ainur sang it into being - the Music of the Ainur - and Eru gave the vision reality with the word 'Ea!'
fields.extra: Melkor wove discord into the Music to dominate it; Eru answered with new themes, declaring that even rebellion would ultimately serve his design.

fields.front: What is the difference between the Valar and the Maiar?
fields.back: Both are Ainur who entered the world; the Valar are the fourteen great powers, the Maiar their lesser kindred
fields.extra: Maiar include Sauron, the Balrogs, Melian, and the five wizards. Melkor was of Valar rank - and mightier than any - but is no longer counted among them.

fields.front: What do the names Melkor and Morgoth mean, and who gave him the second?
fields.back: Melkor is 'He who arises in Might'; Feanor renamed him Morgoth, 'Black Foe of the World'
fields.extra: The name was given after Melkor murdered Feanor's father and stole the Silmarils. The Elves never called him Melkor again.

fields.front: Who is the Elder King, lord of the Valar, and what are his domains?
fields.back: Manwe, lord of winds and airs, dwelling on Taniquetil, the world's highest mountain
fields.extra: Melkor's brother 'in the mind of Eru'. The great Eagles are his servants - every eagle rescue in the books is a nudge from the Elder King.

fields.front: Which Valie (queen of the Valar) kindled the stars, and what do the Elves call her?
fields.back: Varda, called Elbereth Gilthoniel by the Elves
fields.extra: Invoked in the hymn 'A Elbereth Gilthoniel' and in Sam's cry in Shelob's lair. Of all the Valar, Morgoth feared and hated her most.

fields.front: Which Vala, lord of waters, never abandoned the exiled Elves and Men of Middle-earth?
fields.back: Ulmo
fields.extra: He worked through rivers and sea-longing, guiding Turgon to Gondolin and sending Tuor there as his messenger. He dwells in the deeps and takes no spouse.

fields.front: Which Vala created the Dwarves - and why did they survive his mistake?
fields.back: Aule the Smith made the Seven Fathers in secret; when Eru confronted him, Aule offered to destroy them, and Eru adopted them as his children
fields.extra: But they had to sleep until after the Elves, the Firstborn, awoke. Eldest of the Seven Fathers was Durin the Deathless, founder of Khazad-dum.

fields.front: Who asked for the creation of the Ents, and why?
fields.back: Yavanna, Vala of growing things - to protect the trees from Aule's axe-wielding Dwarves and other dangers
fields.extra: Eru granted the Shepherds of the Trees in answer. Yavanna also sang the Two Trees of Valinor into life - her greatest work.

fields.front: Where do the spirits of slain Elves go?
fields.back: The Halls of Mandos in Valinor, where they wait, and may in time be re-embodied
fields.extra: Mandos (properly Namo) is the Doomsman of the Valar. Men's spirits pass through his halls too - but depart the world to a fate unknown.

fields.front: From which Valie did Olorin - the future Gandalf - learn pity?
fields.back: Nienna, the lady of mourning, who grieves for every wound the world suffers
fields.extra: 'Of her he learned pity and patience.' That schooling is why Gandalf spares Gollum - the pity on which the whole quest ultimately turns.

fields.front: Name the Two Trees of Valinor.
fields.back: Telperion the silver and Laurelin the gold
fields.extra: They waxed and waned in alternation, giving Valinor its light before Sun or Moon existed. Telperion was the elder - the White Tree of Gondor descends from his image.

fields.front: Where did the Sun and Moon come from?
fields.back: The last fruit of Laurelin became the Sun; the last flower of Telperion became the Moon
fields.extra: Salvaged after Morgoth and Ungoliant killed the Trees. The Moon rose first - so the Elves, who woke under stars, love moonlight and starlight best.

fields.front: Who helped Morgoth destroy the Two Trees, and what was she?
fields.back: Ungoliant, a primeval spirit in great spider form who drank the Trees' light and poisoned them
fields.extra: Afterward she demanded the Silmarils too, and nearly killed Morgoth - his scream summoned the Balrogs to drive her off. Shelob is her last child.

fields.front: Where did the Elves first awaken?
fields.back: At Cuivienen, the Water of Awakening, under starlight in the far east of Middle-earth
fields.extra: They named themselves the Quendi, 'those who speak with voices'. The Vala Orome found them; Melkor found some first - a darker thread taken up later.

fields.front: Name the three kindreds of the Eldar who set out on the Great Journey to Valinor.
fields.back: The Vanyar, the Noldor, and the Teleri
fields.extra: Those who refused the summons are the Avari, 'the Unwilling'. Teleri who lingered in Beleriand became the Sindar, the Grey Elves - Legolas's people are of that stock.

fields.front: Who was Feanor, and what does his name mean?
fields.back: The greatest craftsman and most gifted of the Noldor, eldest son of King Finwe; his name means 'Spirit of Fire'
fields.extra: He made the Silmarils and the palantiri and devised the Tengwar script. Also proud, possessive, and ruinous - the engine of the First Age's tragedy.

fields.front: What are the Silmarils?
fields.back: Three jewels made by Feanor holding the blended light of the Two Trees - the only such light left after the Trees died
fields.extra: Varda hallowed them so that no evil or mortal flesh could touch them unburned. The entire First Age is 'the war for the Silmarils'.

fields.front: What double crime did Melkor commit at Formenos?
fields.back: He murdered Finwe, High King of the Noldor - the first slaying in the Blessed Realm - and stole the Silmarils
fields.extra: Finwe was Feanor's father. Morgoth set the jewels in his Iron Crown, and their touch burned his hands black forever.

fields.front: What did Feanor and his seven sons swear after the theft of the Silmarils?
fields.back: A terrible oath to pursue with vengeance anyone - Vala, demon, Elf, or Man - who withheld a Silmaril from them
fields.extra: Sworn by the name of Eru himself, it could never be revoked. The Oath drives nearly every catastrophe that follows, including elf-on-elf slaughter.

fields.front: What was the first Kinslaying?
fields.back: The Noldor slew the Teleri at Alqualonde to seize their swan-ships for the pursuit of Morgoth
fields.extra: The Teleri refused to lend the ships. For this the Doom of Mandos was laid on the exiles: treason, ruin, and the Valar's ban.

fields.front: After Feanor burned the ships at Losgar, how did Fingolfin's host reach Middle-earth?
fields.back: They crossed the Helcaraxe, the Grinding Ice of the far north, on foot
fields.extra: Feanor stranded his half-brother's people out of spite. Many died on the ice - among the survivors was Galadriel, who made the crossing herself.

fields.front: How did Feanor die?
fields.back: Mortally wounded by Gothmog, Lord of Balrogs, soon after reaching Middle-earth; his fiery spirit burned his body to ash as it fled
fields.extra: He died within sight of Angband, knowing his war was hopeless - and still bound his sons to the Oath with his last breath.

fields.front: In what land were the wars of the First Age fought, and where is it now?
fields.back: Beleriand, in the northwest of Middle-earth - it was broken and drowned beneath the sea at the age's end
fields.extra: Only fragments remain by Frodo's time, including Lindon and the Blue Mountains. The Shire sits on land that was east of Beleriand entirely.

fields.front: Who ruled the hidden kingdom of Doriath?
fields.back: Thingol, king of the Sindar, and Melian, a Maia - the only marriage of an Ainu and an Elf
fields.extra: Their realm was protected by the Girdle of Melian, an enchanted barrier, with its capital in the cave-city Menegroth, the Thousand Caves.

fields.front: Which High King of the Noldor rode alone to Angband and challenged Morgoth to single combat?
fields.back: Fingolfin - he wounded Morgoth seven times before being crushed by the great hammer Grond
fields.extra: Morgoth limped ever after, and Thorondor, King of Eagles, marred his face while rescuing the body. It is the only time Morgoth was fought hand to hand.

fields.front: How was Maedhros, eldest son of Feanor, rescued from Thangorodrim?
fields.back: Fingon climbed to him with Thorondor's help and cut off his hand to free him from the shackle
fields.extra: Morgoth had hung him from a cliff by the right wrist. The rescue healed the feud between the houses of Feanor and Fingolfin - for a while.

fields.front: What bride-price did Thingol demand of the mortal Beren for his daughter Luthien's hand?
fields.back: A Silmaril, cut from Morgoth's own crown
fields.extra: Meant as an impossible death sentence - and they did it. Luthien sang Morgoth's whole court to sleep while Beren cut a Silmaril free.

fields.front: Who was Luthien Tinuviel?
fields.back: Daughter of Thingol and Melian, accounted the most beautiful of all the Children of Iluvatar - and the mightiest, whose song overthrew both Sauron and Morgoth
fields.extra: Aragorn sings her lay on Weathertop, and Arwen is her image returned. Tolkien had 'Luthien' carved on his wife Edith's gravestone, and 'Beren' on his own.

fields.front: Which elven king died in a werewolf's jaws to save Beren?
fields.back: Finrod Felagund, king of Nargothrond, Galadriel's brother
fields.extra: Captured after losing a duel of songs with Sauron, he broke his bonds and killed the wolf barehanded, fulfilling his oath to Beren's house.

fields.front: What was Sauron in the First Age, before he became the Dark Lord?
fields.back: Morgoth's greatest lieutenant - called Gorthaur the Cruel, master of werewolves on the isle of Tol-in-Gaurhoth
fields.extra: Originally a Maia of Aule the Smith, which explains the ring-craft. Luthien and the hound Huan defeated him and took his isle.

fields.front: Who was Huan, and what fate was decreed for him?
fields.back: The great Hound of Valinor, permitted to speak three times, fated to die only before the mightiest wolf that would ever live
fields.extra: He defeated Sauron in wolf-shape, then met his doom slaying Carcharoth, the monstrous wolf of Angband - both prophecies fulfilled at once.

fields.front: How did Beren lose his hand?
fields.back: The great wolf Carcharoth bit it off at Angband's gate - swallowing the Silmaril it held
fields.extra: The hallowed jewel burned the wolf mad from within. Beren was after called Erchamion, the One-handed; the Silmaril was cut from the wolf's belly as Beren lay dying.

fields.front: What did Luthien's song before Mandos achieve - something never done before or since?
fields.back: It moved Mandos to pity: Beren and Luthien were both returned to life, Luthien accepting mortality as the price
fields.extra: The first elf to truly die and leave the world. Every mortal-immortal love in the legendarium - Aragorn and Arwen above all - lives in this story's shadow.

fields.front: Who slew Glaurung, father of dragons - and what tragedy defined his life?
fields.back: Turin Turambar, cursed by Morgoth: he unknowingly married his own sister Nienor, and both took their own lives when the truth was revealed
fields.extra: He stabbed Glaurung from below with the black sword Gurthang. 'Turambar' means Master of Doom - the bitterest irony in Tolkien. His tale is The Children of Hurin.

fields.front: What did Hurin cry as he held the rearguard alone at the Battle of Unnumbered Tears?
fields.back: 'Aure entuluva!' - 'Day shall come again!' - once for each of the seventy trolls he slew
fields.extra: Captured alive, he was set in a chair on Thangorodrim, cursed to watch Morgoth's doom unfold on his children through the Dark Lord's own eyes.

fields.front: What is the Nirnaeth Arnoediad?
fields.back: The Battle of Unnumbered Tears - the catastrophic fifth battle of Beleriand, where the united Elves, Men, and Dwarves were destroyed by Morgoth
fields.extra: Lost partly through the treachery of Easterling allies. After it, Morgoth's victory in Beleriand was effectively total; only the hidden realms remained.

fields.front: What was Gondolin, and who founded it?
fields.back: The hidden city of King Turgon, built in a secret mountain-ringed vale at Ulmo's prompting - concealed from Morgoth for nearly 400 years
fields.extra: The greatest elven city of the age. Glamdring, Orcrist, and Sting were all forged there - which is why Third Age orcs still recognize the blades.

fields.front: Who betrayed Gondolin's location to Morgoth, and why?
fields.back: Maeglin, Turgon's nephew - captured and broken, but also lured by the promise of the city and of Idril, the king's daughter, whom he coveted
fields.extra: During the Fall he tried to seize Idril and her son; her husband Tuor threw him from the walls. Tolkien's archetypal traitor-from-within.

fields.front: What did Glorfindel do during the escape from the Fall of Gondolin?
fields.back: He fought a Balrog on a mountain pass to save the refugees, killing it as they both fell into the abyss
fields.extra: Re-embodied in Valinor and sent back to Middle-earth, he is the same Glorfindel who faces the Nazgul at the Ford of Bruinen two ages later.

fields.front: Who sailed to Valinor to plead for mercy for Elves and Men, and what became of him?
fields.back: Earendil the Mariner, with a Silmaril bound on his brow - the Valar set him in the sky, where he sails forever as the evening star
fields.extra: Son of Tuor and Idril of Gondolin, husband of Elwing, granddaughter of Beren and Luthien - both great lines meet in him. His ship is Vingilot.

fields.front: What light shines in the Phial of Galadriel that Frodo carries into Shelob's lair?
fields.back: The light of Earendil's star - which is the light of a Silmaril, and through it the last light of the Two Trees of Valinor
fields.extra: The deck's longest thread: Two Trees to Silmaril to Earendil to Galadriel's mirror-water to a hobbit in a spider's den.

fields.front: How did the First Age end?
fields.back: The War of Wrath: the host of the Valar answered Earendil's plea, destroyed Morgoth's armies, and thrust him through the Door of Night into the Void
fields.extra: The cataclysm broke and drowned Beleriand. Morgoth remains outside the world - prophecy says until the Last Battle.

fields.front: Who was the greatest dragon that ever lived, and who slew him?
fields.back: Ancalagon the Black, first and mightiest of the winged dragons - slain by Earendil in the sky during the War of Wrath
fields.extra: His fall broke the towers of Thangorodrim. Gandalf's yardstick for the One Ring: not even Ancalagon's fire could have harmed it.

fields.front: What was the final fate of the three Silmarils?
fields.back: One sails the sky with Earendil; Maedhros carried one into a fiery chasm; Maglor cast the last into the sea
fields.extra: The recovered jewels burned the last two sons of Feanor - the Oath had made them evil-doers, and the hallowed gems rejected them. One in air, one in earth, one in water.

fields.front: What choice did the Valar grant the Half-elven, and how did Elrond and Elros choose?
fields.back: To belong either to the Elves or to Men: Elrond chose the Elves; his twin Elros chose mortality and founded Numenor
fields.extra: Elrond's children inherit the choice, delayed until his departure - the whole weight behind Arwen's decision to stay for Aragorn.

fields.front: According to The Silmarillion, where did the Orcs come from?
fields.back: They were bred by Melkor from Elves captured at Cuivienen, corrupted and ruined in mockery of the Children of Iluvatar
fields.extra: So the Silmarillion's wise hold - Tolkien himself kept revisiting the question and never settled it. Melkor cannot create, only corrupt.

fields.front: Name Melkor's two great fortresses.
fields.back: Utumno, his first, destroyed when the Valar captured him; and Angband, his second, beneath the towers of Thangorodrim
fields.extra: Sauron commanded Angband during his master's captivity. Every later dark tower - Dol Guldur, Barad-dur - is an echo of these.
